label: Chapter-9.ppt
title: Chapter 11 Chapter 9: C++ Arrays and Strings

questions.text: What is the primary characteristic of a **structured data type** in C++?

choices.label: It can store only one value at a time.
choices.text: It can store only one value at a time.

choices.label: It is a collection of other data items.
choices.text: It is a collection of other data items.

choices.label: It consists only of integral data types.
choices.text: It consists only of integral data types.

choices.label: It is always passed by value to functions.
choices.text: It is always passed by value to functions.

questions.explanation: As per the text, a structured data type is one in which each data item is a collection of other data items, whereas a simple data type stores only one value at a time.

questions.text: In C++, array indexing always starts at which value?

choices.label: Any integer defined by the user
choices.text: Any integer defined by the user

questions.explanation: In C++, array indices are zero-based, meaning the first element is at index 0.

questions.text: What happens if a program attempts to access an array index that is **out of bounds** in C++?

choices.label: The compiler generates a syntax error.
choices.text: The compiler generates a syntax error.

choices.label: The program automatically resizes the array.
choices.text: The program automatically resizes the array.

choices.label: C++ does not guard against this; it may cause data corruption or crashes.
choices.text: C++ does not guard against this; it may cause data corruption or crashes.

choices.label: The index is automatically wrapped around using the modulo operator.
choices.text: The index is automatically wrapped around using the modulo operator.

questions.explanation: C++ does not perform automatic bounds checking. Accessing an index outside the valid range [0, SIZE-1] leads to undefined behavior.

questions.text: Which of the following is an **illegal** aggregate operation on arrays `list1` and `list2` (both of size 25)?
questions.explanation: C++ does not allow aggregate assignment between arrays. You must copy elements one by one using a loop.

questions.text: When an array is passed as a parameter to a function, how is it transferred?

choices.label: By pointer only (the `&` symbol is required)
choices.text: By pointer only (the `&` symbol is required)

choices.label: Only if the size is specified in the formal parameter
choices.text: Only if the size is specified in the formal parameter

questions.explanation: Arrays are always passed by reference in C++. The base address of the array is passed to the function.

questions.text: What is the special character used to terminate a C-string?
questions.explanation: C-strings are null-terminated character arrays, ending with the null character `\0`.

questions.text: If you declare `char name[16];`, what is the maximum number of characters you can store in the string while remaining valid?
questions.explanation: Because one space is required for the null terminator (`\0`), a character array of size 16 can hold a string of at most 15 characters.

questions.text: Which function is used to read a C-string that contains blanks/spaces?
questions.explanation: The standard extraction operator `>>` stops at whitespace. To read blanks, `cin.get` or `cin.getline` is used.

questions.text: In a two-dimensional array declaration `int matrix[5][10];`, what do 5 and 10 represent?
questions.explanation: The first dimension in a 2D array declaration specifies the number of rows, and the second dimension specifies the number of columns.

questions.text: What is the 'base address' of an array?

choices.label: The address of the last component
choices.text: The address of the last component

choices.label: The total memory size of the array
choices.text: The total memory size of the array

choices.label: The memory location of the first array component
choices.text: The memory location of the first array component

choices.label: The number of elements in the array
choices.text: The number of elements in the array

questions.explanation: The base address is the address of the first element (index 0) of the array.

questions.text: Consider the code: `int list[5] = {2, 4, 6};`. What is the value of `list[3]`?
questions.explanation: When an array is partially initialized during declaration, C++ initializes the remaining elements to 0.
The loop runs for `i = 0, 1, 2`. It stops when `i` reaches 3 (which is not less than 3). Thus, it prints `val[0]`, `val[1]`, and `val[2]`.
The loop starts at `i = 1`. `arr[1]` is 10, and `arr[2]` is 15. `sum = 10 + 15 = 25`. Note that `arr[0]` (5) is skipped.

questions.text: Given the declaration `char str[] = "C++";`, what is the value of `sizeof(str)`?
questions.explanation: The string "C++" contains 3 characters ('C', '+', '+'). C++ automatically adds the null terminator '\0' at the end, making the total size 4.
`table[1]` refers to the second row `{4, 5, 6}`. `table[1][0]` refers to the first element of that second row, which is 4.
The loop shifts elements to the left: `list[0]` becomes `list[1]` (2), `list[1]` becomes `list[2]` (3), etc. The last element `list[4]` is never modified by the loop, so it remains 5.

questions.text:
In the following function prototype, which dimension of the 2D array **must** be specified?

`void process(int board[][?], int rows);`
questions.explanation: When a 2D array is passed to a function, the first dimension can be omitted, but the second dimension (columns) must be specified so the compiler can calculate memory offsets.
`greeting[0]` is 'H', `greeting[1]` was 'i'. Changing `greeting[1]` to 'o' results in the string "Ho".
The algorithm initializes `max` with the first element (12). It then compares 45 (new max), then 7 (no change), then 33 (no change). The result is 45.

questions.text: If `int a[5] = {0};` and `int b[5] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};`, what happens after executing `for(int i=0; i<5; i++) a[i] = b[i];`?

choices.label: A runtime error occurs because of aggregate assignment.
choices.text: A runtime error occurs because of aggregate assignment.

questions.explanation: Since the assignment is done element-by-element inside a loop (component-wise), it is perfectly legal and copies the contents of `b` into `a`.
The loop accesses diagonal elements: `matrix[0][0]` (1) and `matrix[1][1]` (4). `1 + 4 = 5`.

questions.text: Which of the following describes **parallel arrays**?

choices.label: Arrays that are sorted in the same order.
choices.text: Arrays that are sorted in the same order.

choices.label: Two or more arrays where corresponding components hold related information.
choices.text: Two or more arrays where corresponding components hold related information.

choices.label: Arrays that share the same memory address.
choices.text: Arrays that share the same memory address.

choices.label: A single array that can be processed in two directions.
choices.text: A single array that can be processed in two directions.

questions.explanation: Parallel arrays are used to store related data, such as a student ID in one array and their corresponding grade in another at the same index.

questions.text: What does `cout << name;` do if `name` is a character array that was never null-terminated?

choices.label: It prints only the first character.
choices.text: It prints only the first character.

choices.label: It outputs characters from memory until a null character is eventually found.
choices.text: It outputs characters from memory until a null character is eventually found.

questions.explanation: `cout` for character arrays continues reading memory sequentially until it hits a `\0` byte. If the array lacks one, it will read adjacent (garbage) memory.

questions.text: What is the result of the following string comparison: `"Hello" < "hello"`?
questions.explanation: In ASCII, uppercase letters have lower numerical values than lowercase letters. Therefore, 'H' (72) is less than 'h' (104).

questions.text: How do you find the sum of the **third row** (index 2) of `int matrix[5][10]`?
questions.explanation: To process a specific row, the row index remains constant (2), and you iterate through all column indices (0 to 9).
